text: The surgeon performs a robotic-assisted bilateral nerve-sparing radical prostatectomy. The endopelvic fascia is incised, and the pelvic floor musculature is reflected off the lateral aspect of the prostate, enhancing its mobility towards the apex. The prostatic component of the puboprostatic ligaments is divided. On the patient's left side, sharp and blunt dissection techniques are used to mobilize the pelvic floor musculature. The dorsal venous complex is secured with a suture.

A retracting suture is applied to the divided end of the uracus for hemostatic control. Cofalid retraction is used to facilitate dissection, and the anterior bladder neck is divided, exposing the supraprostatic urethra. The dissection aims to create a small bladder neck to ease the vesico-urethral anastomosis.

The surgeon continues dissection of the bladder neck to fully expose the supraprostatic urethra, dividing the bladder neck and using a castor for retraction. The posterior bladder neck is divided, and dissection proceeds to identify the vasa in the midline and seminal vesicles laterally. Hemlock clips are applied to control the bladder wings. The right vas deferens is identified, retracted, and clipped along with the artery to the apex of the seminal vesicle, lifting the seminal vesicles to access the posterior plane of the prostate. The same procedure is repeated on the left side.

The surgeon retracts both vasa deferentia towards the anterior abdominal wall, creating a posterior plane between the prostate and the anterior leaflet of Denonvilliers' fascia. The rectum is visible behind this fascial layer.

The surgeon uses a robotic approach with a thirty-degree camera to retract and peel away Denonvier's fascia from the posterior surface of the prostate. The right vascular pedicle is controlled, allowing identification of the prostatic capsule and careful dissection of the neurovascular bundle on the right side. An intrafacial dissection is performed using a combination of sharp and blunt techniques, avoiding cautery near the neurovascular bundle to aid early functional recovery. The dissection continues down to the apex of the prostate, with the same steps repeated on the left side.

The surgeon controls the proximal vascular pedicle using hemlock clips, revealing the posterolateral contour of the prostate. The neurovascular bundle is dissected free in an a-thermal manner, continuing down towards the apex of the prostate. The lateral prostatic fascia is released, and once the neurovascular bundle is freed on both sides, apical dissection is performed. The previously secured dorsal venous complex is divided, allowing controlled dissection of the apex.

The assistant retracts the neurovascular bundle laterally on the right side, revealing the urethra after the dorsal venous complex is fully divided. The surgeon transects the urethra, withdraws the catheter, and inspects the prostate before placing it in a specimen bag. Hemostasis is achieved using a 3-0 monocryl suture, followed by the application of Flowseal, a topical hemostatic agent. After a period of observation, the surgeon performs the anastomosis between the bladder neck and urethra using a barbed suture.

The bladder is maneuvered down towards the urethra, and the posterior wall of the anastomosis is constructed following a posterior sphincteropexy. The anastomosis is extended around the left lateral aspect of the bladder and urethra, continuing to the anterior aspect. A second suture is used to complete the anastomosis from the right side, joining with the suture on the anterior left aspect.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis and places reinforcing sutures on the anterior aspect of the bladder, approximating them onto the dorsal venous complex to strengthen the anastomosis.
